text:
The 180-degree shutter rule:

24fps → 1/50 shutter
30fps → 1/60 shutter
60fps → 1/120 shutter

This creates the natural motion blur that makes footage feel cinematic.

Learn more camera fundamentals with FrameCoach 👉 https://framecoach.io

#cinematography #filmtips

You don't need a $5,000 camera to make great films.

You need:
- Good lighting (window light is free)
- Clean audio ($20 lav mic)
- Intentional composition
- A coach for camera settings

FrameCoach handles that last one → https://framecoach.io

#indiefilm #filmmaking

Shallow depth of field is the fastest way to make footage look cinematic.

Open your aperture (f/1.4-f/2.8)
Use a longer lens (50-85mm)
Separate subject from background

FrameCoach coaches you through these decisions on set.

https://framecoach.io

#smartphonefilmmaking #filmmaker

White balance tip:

Never use auto for filmmaking.

☀️ Daylight: 5600K
💡 Tungsten: 3200K
📱 Fluorescent: 4000K
☁️ Cloudy: 6500K

Set it once per scene. Lock it.

More tips → https://framecoach.io

#filmmaking #cinematography

Every filmmaker should know these shot sizes:

• EWS — Establishes location
• WS — Shows body language
• MS — The workhorse shot
• CU — Captures emotion
• ECU — Isolates detail

FrameCoach coaches you on framing in real-time.

https://framecoach.io

#filmtips

I made a list of the 15 biggest mistakes beginner filmmakers make.

#1? Bad audio. Not bad camera.

A $20 lav mic does more for your film than a $2000 lens.

Full list + fixes on our blog → https://theshumba.github.io/framecoach-blog/filmmaking-tips-for-beginners/

#filmmaking

Rule of thirds is great.

But knowing WHEN TO BREAK IT is what makes you a filmmaker.

Center framing = power (Kubrick)
Extreme off-center = tension
Dynamic center = natural feel

Learn composition on set with FrameCoach → https://framecoach.io

#composition

The $0 lighting setup that looks professional:

1. Window (key light)
2. White foam board ($3) — bounce fill
3. Black foam board ($3) — negative fill

That's it. $6 total. Better than most beginner 3-light kits.

More free filmmaking tips → https://framecoach.io

#lighting

Want to make your first short film this weekend?

1. Write a 60-second story (no dialogue)
2. Plan 5-8 shots
3. Shoot each 3 times
4. Edit in DaVinci Resolve (free)
5. Add music from YouTube Audio Library

For camera coaching → https://framecoach.io

#shortfilm

The exposure triangle for filmmakers, simplified:

1. Shutter speed: LOCK IT (1/50 at 24fps)
2. Aperture: CREATIVE CHOICE (depth of field)
3. ISO: LAST RESORT (keep low for clean image)

FrameCoach walks you through this on set → https://framecoach.io

#filmmaking

Color tells stories before dialogue does.

Red = danger, passion
Blue = isolation, cold
Gold = nostalgia, warmth
Green = nature, sickness

Plan your color palette as carefully as your script.

Learn visual storytelling → https://framecoach.io

#colortheory #filmmaking

What they did: made films constantly, studied other films obsessively, and learned by doing.

#filmmaking #selftaught

Camera settings for a cinematic dialogue scene:

24fps | 1/50 shutter | f/1.4-f/2.0
85mm lens | Manual WB | Low ISO

This gives you shallow DOF, natural motion blur, and flattering close-ups.

Real-time coaching → https://framecoach.io

#cinematography

Negative space in film is not empty space.

It's isolation.
It's tension.
It's breathing room.
It's storytelling.

The courage to leave your frame empty is the same courage that makes great films.

Learn composition → https://framecoach.io

#filmmaker #filmmakingtips

The best B-roll isn't pretty — it's meaningful.

Hands trembling = emotional state
Empty chair = someone missing
Clock on wall = time pressure
Photo on shelf = memory

Every shot should communicate, not just decorate.

Solo filmmaker checklist before hitting record:

☐ Frame rate: 24fps
☐ Shutter: 1/50
☐ White balance: manual
☐ Focus: locked
☐ Audio: recording
☐ Composition: rule of thirds

Or just use FrameCoach 😉

Your lens choice is a storytelling decision:

Wide (24mm) = energy, distortion, immersion
Normal (50mm) = natural, neutral, invisible
Telephoto (85mm) = intimate, compressed, voyeuristic

Match the lens to the emotion.

The fastest way to make footage look cinematic:

1. Shoot at 24fps
2. Open aperture for shallow DOF
3. Light from the side (not front)
4. Use a longer lens (50mm+)
5. Grade in post (reduce saturation 10-15%)

FrameCoach coaches steps 1-4 live.

https://framecoach.io

Every indie filmmaker should learn these 3 things before buying ANY gear:

1. How light works (direction, quality, color)
2. How composition works (framing, depth, balance)
3. How audio works (proximity, room treatment)

Start with #1 and #2 → https://framecoach.io

Auto mode on your camera:

❌ Shifts ISO mid-shot
❌ Changes white balance between takes
❌ Hunts for focus on the wrong subject
❌ Makes your footage impossible to edit consistently

Manual mode + FrameCoach coaching = consistency.

https://framecoach.io

#filmmaking

Documentary tip: the best interview footage happens in the silence AFTER the answer.

Ask your question. Listen. Wait.

People fill silence with their deepest thoughts.

More filmmaking tips → https://framecoach.io

#documentary #filmmaking #interview

Camera settings for golden hour:

☀️ WB: 5200-5600K (accurate) or 6000K+ (enhanced warmth)
🎞️ 24fps, 1/50 shutter
📷 f/2.0-f/4 (beautiful backlight bokeh)
🔅 ISO: native

You have ~30 minutes. Plan every shot.

Camera coaching → https://framecoach.io

#goldenhour

Film noir lighting on $0 budget:

1. One hard light source (desk lamp)
2. Black card on the shadow side
3. No fill light at all
4. High contrast, deep shadows
5. Shoot at night or in a dark room

The moodiest lighting is the cheapest.

https://framecoach.io

#filmnoir

Kubrick used symmetry.
Anderson uses it too.
Villeneuve uses it for awe.

Center your subject.
Level the camera perfectly.
Let the frame speak.

Break the rule of thirds with intention.

Learn composition → https://framecoach.io

No. You'll start filmmaking when you decide to start.

#filmmaking

The 3-lens filmmaker kit:

1. 24mm or 35mm (wide) — establishing shots, environments
2. 50mm (normal) — the workhorse, natural perspective
3. 85mm (telephoto) — close-ups, portraits, bokeh

These 3 cover 90% of filmmaking situations.

https://framecoach.io

#filmmaker #lenses

Composition hack: shoot THROUGH things.

Doorframes. Plants. Crowd shoulders. Window bars. Fence gaps.

Adding a foreground element instantly creates depth and makes any shot look 10x more professional.

More composition tips → https://framecoach.io

Camera settings cheat sheet for beginners:

Narrative: 24fps, 1/50, f/1.4-2.8, native ISO
Documentary: 24fps, 1/50, f/2.8-5.6, auto ISO ok
Action: 24fps, 1/50, f/2.8-4, wide lens
Interview: 24fps, 1/50, f/2.0, 85mm

→ https://framecoach.io

#filmmaking

Audio tip that saves every beginner filmmaker:

Bad audio quality makes your film feel amateur.
Good audio quality makes your film feel professional.

A $20 lav mic > $2000 lens upgrade.

Prioritize: audio → lighting → camera → everything else.

Making a short film this weekend? Here's your camera checklist:

1. Set 24fps
2. Set 1/50 shutter speed
3. Set white balance manually
4. Choose aperture for depth of field
5. Set ISO last
6. Lock focus
7. Roll

Or let FrameCoach guide you → https://framecoach.io

Leading lines are everywhere.

Roads. Hallways. Shadows. Fences. Light beams.

Find them. Point them at your subject.

Instantly better composition.

Learn more visual techniques → https://framecoach.io

Color grading in 60 seconds:

1. Match exposure across shots
2. Correct white balance
3. Lower highlights -20
4. Lift shadows +10
5. Desaturate -15%
6. Add warm midtones, cool shadows
7. Subtle vignette

DaVinci Resolve does this for free.

https://framecoach.io

#colorgrading

Every frame is a choice.

Where you put the camera.
What lens you use.
What's in focus.
What's in the frame.
What's NOT in the frame.

Great filmmakers make these choices intentionally.

FrameCoach coaches you to make better choices.
